seo.title: Enterprise SaaS Code Generation AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for code generation AI agents in enterprise saas. Covers SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, GDPR.

content.intro: Deploying AI code generation agents in Enterprise SaaS environments introduces compounded risk across security, compliance, and operational reliability — where a single misconfiguration can trigger SLA breaches, data exposure across tenants, or contract termination. SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, GDPR, and CCPA obligations require that every AI-assisted action is auditable, tenant-isolated, and data-residency-compliant. This checklist provides concrete, actionable controls to safely deploy LLM-based code generation agents with sandbox execution in high-sensitivity enterprise environments.

sections.heading: Sandbox Execution & Code Safety Controls
sections.description: Ensure all LLM-generated code is executed in isolated, ephemeral environments with strict resource and network controls before any output is accepted or deployed.

items.label: Ephemeral Sandbox Isolation per Execution
items.description: Provision a fresh, isolated container or VM for each code execution request using tools like gVisor, Firecracker, or Docker with seccomp profiles. Destroy the environment immediately after execution completes to prevent state leakage between runs.

items.label: Network Egress Blocking in Sandbox
items.description: Apply network policies that block all outbound internet traffic from the sandbox execution environment by default. Only allowlist specific internal endpoints required for test validation, and log every attempted connection.

items.label: Static Analysis Before Execution
items.description: Run generated code through static analysis tools (e.g., Semgrep, Bandit for Python, ESLint security plugins for JS) to detect dangerous patterns such as eval(), exec(), subprocess calls, or file system writes before sandbox execution begins.

items.label: CPU, Memory, and Execution Time Limits
items.description: Enforce hard resource quotas per sandbox execution: maximum 30-second wall-clock timeout, 512MB memory cap, and single-core CPU limit. Terminate and flag any execution that exceeds these thresholds as a potential denial-of-service attempt.

items.label: Dependency Vulnerability Scanning
items.description: Before installing any packages referenced in generated code, scan them against OSV, Snyk, or GitHub Advisory Database. Block installation of packages with critical CVEs or packages not present in a pre-approved enterprise dependency allowlist.

items.label: Output Diff Review Gate
items.description: Require a human-in-the-loop approval step or automated policy gate before any AI-generated code is merged into production branches. Enforce this via CI/CD pipeline rules that reject commits tagged as AI-generated without a passing review token.

items.label: Iterative Refinement Loop Depth Limit
items.description: Cap the number of LLM refinement iterations per code generation task at a configurable maximum (e.g., 5 iterations). Log each iteration with its input prompt, output diff, and test result to prevent runaway agent loops and ensure full auditability.

sections.heading: Credential & Sensitive Data Leakage Prevention
sections.description: Prevent the LLM agent from ingesting, generating, or transmitting credentials, secrets, PII, or proprietary customer data through code outputs, prompts, or execution artifacts.

items.label: Pre-Prompt Credential Scrubbing
items.description: Scan all user-supplied context and code snippets passed to the LLM using regex and entropy-based detection (e.g., detect-secrets, truffleHog patterns) to redact API keys, tokens, passwords, and connection strings before they reach the model.

items.label: LLM Output Credential Pattern Detection
items.description: Apply a post-generation filter on all LLM outputs that detects and blocks responses containing high-entropy strings, known secret patterns (AWS keys, GitHub tokens, JWT secrets), or hardcoded IP addresses before returning results to the user.

items.label: PII Detection in Code Context
items.description: Integrate a PII detection layer (e.g., Microsoft Presidio, AWS Comprehend) on all code context windows sent to the LLM. Automatically mask or reject requests that include email addresses, SSNs, credit card numbers, or other GDPR/CCPA-regulated data categories.

items.label: Environment Variable Injection Policy
items.description: Enforce a policy that generated code must reference secrets exclusively via environment variables or a secrets manager (e.g., HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager). Automatically reject any generated code that hardcodes credentials, even in test files.

items.label: Sandbox Artifact Purge After Execution
items.description: Ensure all files, logs, environment variables, and memory artifacts created during sandbox execution are cryptographically wiped after each run. Verify purge completion via a post-execution integrity check before releasing the sandbox slot.

items.label: Prompt Injection Attack Detection
items.description: Implement a prompt injection detection layer that scans user-supplied code comments, variable names, and docstrings for attempts to override system instructions or exfiltrate data. Log and block requests that trigger injection signatures.

items.label: LLM Provider Data Processing Agreement
items.description: Ensure a signed Data Processing Agreement (DPA) is in place with your LLM provider that explicitly prohibits training on customer code inputs, guarantees data deletion timelines, and specifies the geographic regions where inference occurs.

sections.heading: Tenant Isolation & Data Residency Compliance
sections.description: Enforce strict logical and physical separation of all AI agent activity, generated artifacts, and audit data between enterprise tenants, aligned with per-customer data residency contractual obligations.

items.label: Per-Tenant Namespace Isolation for Agent Workloads
items.description: Deploy code generation agent workloads in dedicated Kubernetes namespaces or separate cloud accounts per tenant tier. Apply network policies that prevent any cross-namespace communication and validate isolation with automated penetration tests quarterly.

items.label: Data Residency Enforcement at Inference Layer
items.description: Map each enterprise customer's data residency requirements (EU, US, APAC) to specific LLM inference endpoints deployed in compliant regions. Enforce routing at the API gateway layer and alert on any request routed outside the contracted region.

items.label: Tenant-Scoped Audit Log Storage
items.description: Store all AI-assisted code generation audit logs in tenant-specific, encrypted storage buckets with customer-managed encryption keys (CMEK). Ensure logs are not co-mingled across tenants and are exportable by the customer on demand for their own compliance audits.

items.label: Context Window Tenant Tagging
items.description: Tag every LLM prompt and completion with a tenant identifier at the infrastructure level. Implement middleware that validates the tenant tag matches the authenticated session before processing, preventing context bleed between concurrent tenant requests.

items.label: Shared Model Cache Isolation
items.description: If using a shared LLM inference cluster, ensure KV-cache and prompt caching mechanisms are disabled or scoped per tenant to prevent one tenant's code context from being served to another tenant through cache hits.

items.label: Customer-Controlled Data Deletion
items.description: Implement a verified data deletion API that allows enterprise customers to trigger deletion of all their AI-generated code artifacts, prompt history, and audit logs within 30 days, with a signed deletion certificate returned for their compliance records.

items.label: Third-Party Subprocessor Disclosure
items.description: Maintain and publish an up-to-date list of all subprocessors involved in the code generation pipeline (LLM providers, sandbox vendors, logging services). Notify enterprise customers at least 30 days before adding new subprocessors, as required by GDPR Article 28.

sections.heading: Audit Logging, SLA Monitoring & Incident Response
sections.description: Establish comprehensive, tamper-evident audit trails for all AI agent actions and implement proactive monitoring to detect SLA-threatening failures, security incidents, and compliance violations before they escalate.

items.label: Immutable Audit Log for All AI-Assisted Actions
items.description: Write append-only, cryptographically signed audit log entries for every code generation request, including: tenant ID, user ID, timestamp, prompt hash, model version, execution result, test outcomes, and human review decision. Store in a WORM-compliant log store (e.g., AWS CloudTrail with log file validation).

items.label: Real-Time SLA Breach Detection
items.description: Instrument the code generation pipeline with latency and availability metrics. Configure alerts that fire when p99 latency exceeds 80% of the SLA threshold or when error rates exceed 0.1% over a 5-minute window, giving the on-call team time to intervene before a breach is recorded.

items.label: Automated Compliance Violation Alerting
items.description: Deploy automated rules in your SIEM (e.g., Splunk, Datadog) that trigger high-priority alerts when audit logs show: credential patterns in outputs, cross-tenant data access attempts, data routed outside contracted regions, or sandbox escape attempts.

items.label: AI Agent Action Attribution in Audit Trail
items.description: Distinguish between human-authored and AI-generated code changes in all audit logs and version control history. Use a dedicated commit signature or metadata tag for AI-assisted commits so that post-incident forensics can accurately attribute changes during SOC 2 audits.

items.label: Incident Response Runbook for AI-Specific Failures
items.description: Maintain a documented incident response runbook that covers AI-specific failure modes: prompt injection attacks, sandbox escapes, credential leakage in generated code, and LLM provider outages. Test the runbook in a tabletop exercise at least twice per year.

items.label: Model Version Pinning and Rollback Procedure
items.description: Pin the LLM model version used in production and document the rollback procedure for reverting to a previous model version within 1 hour if a new version produces unsafe outputs or causes SLA degradation. Log all model version changes with the approving engineer's identity.

items.label: Customer-Facing Audit Log Export API
items.description: Provide enterprise customers with a self-service API to export their complete AI agent audit logs in a standard format (JSON, CSV) for their own SOC 2 and ISO 27001 audits. Ensure exports are available within 24 hours of request and cover the full contractual retention period.

items.label: Quarterly Red Team Exercise for Code Generation Pipeline
items.description: Conduct quarterly adversarial testing of the code generation pipeline, specifically targeting prompt injection, sandbox escape, cross-tenant data access, and credential exfiltration vectors. Document findings and remediation timelines in your ISO 27001 risk register.

notes.regulation: SOC 2 Type II — Availability & Confidentiality Criteria
notes.note: Your AI code generation pipeline must be included in the SOC 2 audit scope. Ensure that sandbox execution infrastructure, LLM API integrations, and audit logging systems are covered by your monitoring controls (CC7.2) and that logical access to AI-generated artifacts is restricted to authorized personnel (CC6.1). Document the AI agent as a system component in your System Description.

notes.regulation: GDPR Article 28 & 32 — Processor Obligations and Security of Processing
notes.note: If any EU customer code context or PII flows through the LLM pipeline, your LLM provider is a data processor under GDPR Article 28 and must have a signed DPA. Article 32 requires you to implement pseudonymization and encryption of personal data at rest and in transit throughout the code generation pipeline, and to conduct a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) given the high-risk automated processing nature of AI code generation.

notes.regulation: CCPA Section 1798.100 — Consumer Rights and Data Minimization
notes.note: For California-based enterprise customers whose employees use the code generation agent, ensure that any personal information incidentally captured in code context (e.g., developer names in comments, email addresses in test fixtures) is subject to your CCPA data inventory. Implement data minimization controls to strip personal information from prompts before LLM submission, and honor deletion requests within the 45-day CCPA response window.

notes.regulation: ISO 27001 Annex A.14 — System Acquisition, Development and Maintenance
notes.note: AI-generated code must be treated as externally sourced software under ISO 27001 Annex A.14.2. This requires security requirements to be established before code generation tasks are initiated, generated code to undergo security review equivalent to third-party code review, and the LLM model itself to be assessed as a supplier relationship under Annex A.15, with documented supplier security assessments and contractual security obligations.

cta.heading: Automate Your Enterprise SaaS Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously monitor your AI code generation pipeline against SOC 2, ISO 27001, GDPR, and CCPA controls — with automated evidence collection, tenant isolation validation, and real-time SLA breach alerting built in.
